text: BOBENHAUSEN. See BABENHAUSEN.
BOBENTHAL, a town of Bavaria, in the Pfalz circle, on the Lauter. Pop. 3,820.

BOBER, a river of Prussia, in Silesia, rising near a v. of the same name, on the flank of the Rehhorn, on the Riesengebirge, 4 m. NW of Schatzlar, in Bohemia, and flowing into the Oder, on the l. bank, at Krossen, in Brandenburg, after a course of 200 m., in which it passes Liebau, Landshut, Hirschberg, Bunzlau, Sagan, Naumburg, and Bobersberg. Its principal affluent is the Queiss, which joins it on the l. bank, near the Sagan. It also receives the Zacken, and the Tschirna, on the l.; and the Sprotta on the r.
BOBERNHEIM, a village of Bavaria, in the Pfalz circle, N of Frankenthal. Pop. 630.
BOBERSBERG, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of Brandenburg, 10 m. S of Krossen, on the Bober. Pop. 1,420.
BOBILI, a town of Hindostan, in the Northern Circars, in N lat. 18° 20', celebrated for its desperate defence against a French force in 1757.
BOBINGEN, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Suabia, on the Sinkel river. Pop. 1,328.

BOBLINGEN, a town of Wurtemberg, in the Neckar circle, 12 m. SW of Stutgard, at an alt. of 1,480 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 3,200. There are chemical works here, and several breweries. BOBR, BOVER, or BIEBEZA, a river of Russia, an affluent of the Nareva, which it joins on the r. bank, above Wizna, after a long and almost torpid course through a level sandy district, in which, after passing Goniandz, it converts an area of above 200 sq. m. into a morass. Its principal affluents are the Netta and the Lyk.
BOBRKA, a town of Austria, in the gov. of Lemberg, in Galicia, 40 m. NW of Brzezan. Pop. 5,148.
BOBROF, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Voronez, on the r. bank of the Bitioug. Pop. 5,500.
BOBRONISK, a town of Russia, in the gov. and 80 m. SE of Minsk, on the Berezina, at the confluence of the Bobroniia. Pop. 5,500. This is a prin- Dnieper and the Berezina.

BOBROWNIKI, a town of Russian Poland, in the gov. of Podlachia, 30 m. NW of Lublin, on the r. bank of the Wieprz. Pop. 1,500. BOCA the Spanish, Bocca the Italian, term for the narrow entrance of a river or a bay. It literally signifies mouth; and is of frequent occurrence in the designation of places visited by the earlier navigators. BOCA CHICA, [i. e., 'Little month' or 'channel,'] the entrance into the harbour of Carthagena, in S America, in N lat. 10° 19′ 30", W long. 75° 36′ 17".-Also the smaller entrance into the bay of Manilla, in the Philippines, lying between the island of Corredegir and the peninsula of Batan.
BOCA DE ALCATRACES, a narrow channel between some small islands near the N coast of Cuba, in N lat.23°.
BOCA DE CANAVERA, a narrow channel between the islands of Quadra and Vancouver, in N lat. 48° 57'.
BOCA DE CARANGERA, the western entrance of the river of Cambodia,
BOCA CAUTIVA, a channel leading into San Carlos bay, on the coast of Florida.
BOCA DE CHIRIQUI, a channel of the Spanish main, which forms the entrance into Chiriqui lagune, on the coast of Panama, in N lat. 8° 50'.
BOCA ESCONDIDA, a bay on the W coast of Yucatan.

BOCA GRANDE, the larger channel leading into the bay of Manilla, on the coast of the island of Luzon, between the island of Sinalan and Pulo Cavallo,—Also a channel in San Carlos bay, on the coast of Florida, affording good anchorage in 18 ft. water. -Also a bay at the mouth of the Zucar, on the SE coast of Costa- Rica, in N lat 10° 50'. BOCA. See also Bocca.
BOCAGE (LE), a district of France, in Normandy, in the dep. of Calvados, between the Orne river and the ocean. Its cap. is Vire.

BOCAINA, a village of Brazil in the prov. of Piauhy, on the Guaribas river.—Also a v. of Minas- Geraes, in the comarca of Rio-da-Parahibuna.—Also a serra or mountain-range in the prov. of Rio-de- Janeiro, a branch of the Organ cordillera.
BOCALIEU. See BACALIEU.
BOCAS CERRADAS, four narrow channels communicating with the Madre lagune on the Tampico coast of Mexico, in N lat. 25°.

BOCAS DEL DRAGO, a series of channels between the island of Trinidad and the continent of America, forming the N entrance into the gulf of Paria. They are four in number, and their navigation is extremely intricate owing to the currents, and the frequent sudden fallings of the wind.
BOCASSE (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, cant. of Cleres. Pop. 658.
BOCAYNA (LA), or CANAL DE BOCAYNA, a channel 6 m. in breadth, between the island of Fuertaventura, in the Canary group, and Lanzarote.
BOCAYRENTE, or BOCAIRENT, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Valencia, 12 m. S of San-Felipe. Pop. 4,070.

BOCCA DI CATTARO, or BOUCHES-DE-CATTARO, the singularly outlined harbour of the town of Cattaro, on the coast of Dalmatia, lying between N lat. 42° 24' and 42° 30'. It consists of a series of bends and reaches, amidst the roots of the Montenegro chain, through which a vessel must steer in a series of short traverses; but the whole affords an excellent and capacious harbour. The coasts are inhabited by a peculiar race of men, known as the Bocchesi, who make admirable sailors, but are a rude, turbulent, semi-barbarous race of men. They are said to number 40,000.

BOCCA TIGRIS, or THE BOGUE, the entrance of the Chukiang river, on the coast of China. To this point the estuary of the river presents a broad expanse, interspersed with islands. The main part of the river flows through the Bogue; but to the W several deltoid branches form shallow channels through which the flat-bottomed trading vessels of the natives can sail to Canton. The Bogue is defended by six forts, three of which are placed immediately in the entrance, and two 2 m. lower down, while another is about 2 m. above, on the W side of 1 BOC cipal station for the steamers which navigate the | the river. The distance across between the two lower forts, which are placed opposite each other, and mounted 10 guns each, when the Bogue was forced in Sept. 1834 by the Imogene and Andromache, is about 3 m. At Wangton the breadth of the river does not exceed two-thirds of a mile; and on both sides of the river at this spot are very formidable looking batteries. A huge black-looking rock, called Tiger island, 2 m. above Wangton, displays another large battery; and as the narrowness of the deep channel obliges every ship to pass close under it, if this fort were properly manned the passage would be quite impracticable. A second bar occurs about 7 m. above Tiger island, and 22 m. from Canton; but the defences upwards to Whampoa, which is the port of Canton, and 10 m. below that city, are quite insignificant.

BOCCHETTA (LA), a mountain and pass of the Northern Apennines, in the Sardinian states, between Genoa and Novi, 15 m. from either of these places. It is the most elevated point in this section of the Apennines: the highest part of the pass being 6,660 ft. above sea-level. From this lofty position there is a noble view of Genoa and its gulf. Two copious streams descend from it; the Polcevera which runs S to the gulf of Genoa; and the Lemma, a still more considerable stream, which runs N to the Orba. The B. is difficult to cross, being so narrow, in some places on the old line of road, that three persons could scarcely walk abreast; and it sometimes required 14 hours to go from Genoa to Novi. A new and far more practicable, though less picturesque, road by Campo Macone has been executed within these few years. The pass is, besides, an important military post, as the key of Italy on the side of Genoa, and is well-defended by several redoubts. It was forced, however, by the Austrians in 1746; by the French in 1796; and in 1799 by the Austrians.
BOCCHIGLIERO, a town of Naples, in Calabria Citra, in the district of Rossano, 6 m. W of Campana. Pop. 3,380.
BOCEQUILLAS, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Segovia, on the l. bank of the Serezuela, 6 m. NE of Sepulveda.

BOCHNIA, a circle or administrative division of Galicia; bounded on the N by Cracow and Poland; on the E by the circle of Tarnov; on the S by the circle of Sandecz; and on the W by that of Myslenice. The Vistula defines its N frontier; the Dunajec its E; on the S a branch of the Carpathians gives it a mountainous character; and in this quarter are extensive and valuable forests, and the productive mines of Wieliczka and Bochnia. In other quarters it is an open grain country varied by low ranges of hills. The superficial area of the circle is 46 German or 977 English sq. m. In 1837, of this surface 191,585 jochs were in cultivation; 76,292, in forests; 45,626, in meadows and gardens; and 47,209 in pasture. The pop. in the same year amounted to 214,006, of whom 6,000 were Jews. The circle contains 5 cities, 9 towns, and 377 villages.

BOCHNIA, the cap. of the above circle, is situated near the Raba or Raab river, 24 m. ESE of Cracow. It is a well-built town, and is surrounded by a range of low argillaceous hills, in which are extensive saltmines, which were first opened in the 13th cent., and at present yield about 15,000 tons of salt annually. The pop. of the town, which is the seat of a boardof-mines, is 4,800.

BOCHOLT, a town of Prussia, in Westphalia, in the regency and 48 m. W of Munster, on the Aa. Pop. 43,646. It has some distilleries, silk and cotton works, and iron-foundries. The environs are rich in iron. There is a fine castle here belonging to the princes of Salm-Salm. - Also a com. and village of Belgium, in Limburg, 2 m. NNW of Bree. Pop. | through the regency of Magdeburg, passing Quedlin- 1,337.
BOCHUM, a town of Prussia, in Westphalia, in the gov. and 33 m. W of Arnsberg. Pop. 3,496. A considerable quantity of hardware goods is manufactured here.
BOCIGAS, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 45 m. SSE of Burgos.

BOCKAU, a town of Saxony, in the circle of Zwickau, 5 m. WNW of Schwarzenberg, at the foot of the Ochsenkopf. Pop. 1,627. It is celebrated for its manufactures of aromatic essences, and has an active trade in dried herbs and medicinal plants. Cobalt, silver, and iron are mined in the neighbourhood.
BOCKENEM, a town of Hanover, in the district and 17 m. SE of Hildesheim, on the Nette. Pop. 2,352.
BOCKENFIELD, a township in Felton p., Northumberland, 8 m. N of Morpeth. Pop. 127.
BOCKENHEIM. See BOUQUENON.
BOCKHOLTZ, a town of Limburg, in the arrond. and 16 m. E of Maestricht. Pop. 903.

BOCKING, a parish and v. in Essex, 2 m. N of Braintree, on the N bank of the Pant. Area of p. 3,800 acres. Pop. 3,437.—The v. forms a suburb of Braintree on the N. Manufactories of silk and crape give employment to a considerable number of the inhabitants.
BOCKLETON, a parish in Herefordshire, 5 m. S of Tenbury. Area 3,300 acres. Pop. 358.
BOCKWORTH, a range of mountains in co. Mayo, extending about 4 m. NE, from a point 11 m. NE of Claggan.
BOCOGNANO, a canton and commune of Corsica, 18 m. NE of Ajaccio. Pop. of cant. in 1831, 4,359; of com. in 1841, 1,902.
BOCONNOC, a parish in Cornwall, 4 m. ENE of Lostwithiel. Area 2,230 acres. Pop. 312. William Pitt, Earl of Chatham, was born at his father's seat in this p., on the 15th of Nov., 1708.

BOCQUA, a village on the Niger, below Kakunda and above Atta. Between this latter town and B., the course of the river is about SW, with several turnings in it, and the breadth varies from 2 to 6 m., and it runs between mountains of considerable height.
BOCQUEHO, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cotes-du-Nord, cant. of Chatelaudren. Pop. 1,780.
BOCQUIAUX, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Aisne, com. of Etaves. Pop. 410. There are manufactories of fine muslins and woollens here.

BOCZA, or Borza, a town of Hungary, in the com. of Liptau, 12 m. SE of Werbicze, on a river of the same name, and at the foot of the Teufelhochzeit, [alt. 2,614 ft.] Pop. 1,174. There are copper-mines in the neighbourhood; and gold was formerly mined here, but its working has for some time been abandoned.
BODAJIK, a town of Hungary, in the com. and 12 m. NW of Stuhlweissenburg, on the Sarviz. There are sulphurous mineral springs here.
BODDEN (THE), an arm of the sea, which separates the island of Rügen from the mainland of Pomerania.
BODDINGTON, a parish in Gloucestershire, 4 m. NW by W of Cheltenham. Area 1,550 acres. Pop. 414.—Also a p. in Northamptonshire, 9 m. SW of Daventry. Area 3,770 acres. Pop. 675.
BODDOM, a fishing village on the coast of Buchan, near Peterhead, close to the promontory called Boddom-head, or more frequently Buchan-ness.

BODE, a river of Germany, which rises in the Harz mountains, in four streams, namely, the Kalte Bode, descending from the Brocken, and the Warme B., the Rap-B., and the Lup-B., rising in the Prussian and Brunswick territories; flows from W to E BOD burg, Groningen, Egeln, and Strasfurt, and flows into the Saale at Kloster-Nieuburg, after a course of 74 m. It is a fine romantic stream in the higher part of its course.

BODEAU, a river and lake of Louisiana, U. S. The river rises in Arkansas, and flows S by W through Lafayette and Clayborne counties; and discharges itself into the lake at its NE extremity, in N lat. 32° 37'.
BODEDERN, a parish in Anglesea, 6 m. E of Holyhead. Pop. 1,085.
BODEFELD, a town of Prussia, in Westphalia, in the gov. and 18 m. SE of Arnsberg. Pop. 750.

BODEGA (LA), a settlement founded by the Russians in 1812, on the W coast of California, in N lat. 38° 19′, 80 m. NW of San Francisco, at the mouth of the Slavinska-Ross. The port is inconvenient and small; and the whole establishment was recently sold by the Russian American company to Captain Suter for 30,000 dollars. The pop., previous to this, was occasionally from 400 to 500.
BODEGNE'E, a commune of Belgium, in the dep. of Liege, 8 m. NE of Huy. Pop. 1,060.
BODEGRAVE, a village of Holland, in the prov. of S Holland, 13 m. SE of Leyden, on the r. bank of the Rhine. Pop. 2,100.
BODENBURG, a town of Brunswick, within the Hanoverian territory, 12 m. S of Hildesheim, on the Lamme. Pop. 800.
BODENFELDE, a town of Hanover, 20 m. NW of Munden, on the r. bank of the Weser. Pop. 1,018. Potash is manufactured here.
BODENHAM, a parish in Herefordshire, 8 m. NNE of Hereford. Area 5,550 acres. Pop. 1,017, of whom 341 were in the township of B.
BODENHEIM, a town of Hesse-Darmstadt, in the prov. of the Rhine, 5 m. NNW of Oppenheim, near the l. bank of the Rhine. Pop. 1,726. Excellent wine is made here.
BODEN-SEE. See Constanz (Lake of).

BODENSTADT, or Podstata, a town of Moravia, the cap. of a seignory of the same name, in the regency of Brunn, and circle of Prerau, 20 m. E of Olmutz. Pop. 1,305. It contains a eastle; and possesses some manufactories of linen. It is of considerable antiquity; and was formerly a town of some importance.
BODENSTOWN, a parish in co. Kildare, 3 m. N by E of Naas. Area 2,831 acres. Pop. 459.
BODENTEICH, a bailiwick and town of Hanover, in the principality and 30 m. SSE of Lüneburg, on the l. bank of the Ilmenau. Pop. of t. 587. It has some linen-manufactories.

BODENWERDER, a town of Hanover, in an insulated portion of the principality of Callenburg, on the Weser, 15 m. SE of Hameln, and 30 m. SSW of Hanover. Pop. 1,415. It possesses some cottonmills, and a considerable trade in linen fabrics and timber.

BODERSWEGER, a village of Baden, in the circle of the Middle Rhine, 5 m. SSW of Bischofsheim, and 6 m. ENE of Strasburg. Pop. 1,102. It possesses a considerable trade in hemp, the produce of the locality.
BODEWRYD, a parochial chapelry in the co. of Anglesea, 3 m. SW of Amlwch. Pop. 32.
BODFARY, a parish partly in Denbighshire, partly in Flintshire, 4 m. NE of Denbigh. Pop. 945.
BODFERIN, a parish in Carnarvonshire, 14 m. SW by S of Pwllheli. Pop. 64.
BODGADFAN, a township in Llangelynin p., in Merionethshire. Pop. 211.
BODHAM, a parish in Norfolk, 3 m. E of Holt. Area 1,680 acres. Pop. 292.
BODICOTT, a chapelry in Adderbury p., in Oxfordshire, 2 m. S of Banbury. Area 1,680 acres. Pop. 729.
BODIDDA, a township in Gyffon p., in Carnarvonshire. Pop. 151.
BODIDRIS, a township in Llanarmon p., Denbighshire. Pop. 106.
BODIGHEIM, a village of Baden, in the circle of the Main and Tauber, 4 m. S of Bucheim, and 31 m. ENE of Heidelburg. Pop. 771. It contains a castle.

BODIGUAS, a tribe of S American Indians, in the republic of New Grenada, and prov. of Sta Marta. They are extremely fierce, and at the period of the Spanish invasion, in combination with the Bondas and Jericobas, opposed a most pertinacious resistance to the conquerors.
BODIHAM, or Bodiam, a parish in Sussex, 7 m. NNE of Battle, on the N bank of the Rother. Area 1,480 acres. Pop. 377.
BODILIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Finisterre, cant. of Landivisian. Pop. 1,741.

BODIO, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Tessin, in the valley of Laventina, near the l. bank of the Tessin, 9 m. NNE of Bellinzona. The river is here crossed by a bridge of curious construction.

BODMANN, a village of the duchy of Baden, in the circle of the Lake, 5 m. SSE of Stockach, at the entrance of the river of that name into the lake of Constanz. Pop. 843. It contains the ruins of a castle, formerly the residence of the lieutenants of the Carlovingian kings, from which the lake takes the name of Bodmann or Boden-See. Pop. 843.

BODMIN, a parish and borough in Cornwall, 25 m. W by N of Plymouth, 21 m. W of Launceston. Area of p. 6,310 acres. Pop. in 1831, 3,782; in 1841, 4,643. The town is nearly in the centre of the co., and consists chiefly of one long street running E and W. The church is a spacious structure, in the later style of English architecture. Bone lace was formerly manufactured here in considerable quantities; but this branch of trade has declined; that of shoemaking affords considerable employment. By the municipal act of 1835, the government of the town is vested in a mayor, 4 aldermen, and 12 councillors. B. is the place of election for the two members for the eastern division of Cornwall; it also unites with the parishes of Lanivet, Lanhydrock, and Helland, in returning two borough-members. The pop. of the parl. borough in 1841 was 5,901. The number of electors in 1842-3 was 405. Under the old regime its 37 corporation-voters enjoyed the same right. The county-sessions and assizes are all now holden in this town: the spring-assizes having been removed from Launceston, and the April sessions from Truro, in 1839. A new hall has been erected in the town for the purpose of the assize-business, with two handsome courts. The grand jury-room, indictmentroom, and other requisite offices, are all within the building. A new mayoralty house, and judge's lodging, was built in 1838. The county-gaol and house-of-correction is situated below the town. It is neat and well-arranged, and contains 177 cells, 15 wards, 16 day-rooms, and 15 airing-yards. Two tread-mills, both connected with the same shaft, and two crank-machines, are attached to the building. The employments of the inmates, though various, are not very productive to the county. The number of prisoners in 1835 was 569. A railway runs from B. to Wadebridge, at the head of Padstow harbour. It is used for passenger traffic, as well as for the transport of ores, coal, and sand.
BODNEY, a parish in Norfolk, 5 m. W by S of Watton. Area 2,590 acres. Pop. 98.

BODOE, a town of Norway, the cap. of the bailiwick of Nordland, at the extremity of a peninsula on the N side of the Salten-fiord. It has a port. Pop. in 1833, 277. It was erected into a town in 1816. BOE
BODONAL, a town of Spain, in Estremadura, in the prov. and 46 m. SSE of Badajoz.

BODONITZA, a town of Greece, in the prov. and 30 m. NNW of Livadia, on a stream which flows into the gulf of Molo or Zeitun, and near the entrance of the pass of Thermopylæ. It is supposed to occupy the site of the ancient Thronium.
BODORGAW. See Eglwys-Ael.
BODOWEEN. See ABERFRAW.

BODROG, a river of Hungary, in the com. of Zemplin, formed by the confluence of the Laborcz, Ondova, and Latorcza, at a point 1 m. to the NE of Zemplin. It flows in a SW direction past Patak and Keresztur, and, after a course of 40 m., unites with the Theiss near the town of Tokay. It forms with the latter river the low island of Bodrogköz.
BODROGH. See BACS.
BODROG-KERESZTUR. See KERESZTUR.
BODROUN, or Bodrun, a town of Asia Minor, in the sanjak of Sagla, on the N side of the gulf of Scala Nova, 27 m. SW of Smyrna. It is supposed to occupy the site of the ancient Teos.
BODUAYN, a town of Burmah, on the Myeenguga or Myit-nge, an affluent of the Irawaddi. In the environs are mines of gold and silver.

BODUNGEN (GROSS), a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Saxony, in the regency and 42 m. NW of Erfurth, and 10 m. W of Nordhausen, on the Wipper. Pop. 800. It contains a castle, and has manufactories of woollen stuffs, linen, and potash.
BODWROG, a parochial chapelry in the co. of Anglesea, 10 m. E by S of Holyhead. Pop. 358.
BODZANOWITZ, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of Silesia, gov. of Oppeln, in the circle and 12 m. ENE of Rosenburg, near the Liszwarta. Pop. 700. In the environs is an iron-mine.
BODZANOWO, a town of Poland, in the obwod and 15 m. ESE of Plock, on an affluent of the Vistula. Pop. 233.
BODZECIN, a town of Poland, in the gov. of Sandomir, 32 m. SSW of Radom. It contains a palace belonging to the bishop of Cracow, and about 135 houses.

BOE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Lot-et- Garonne, cant. of Agen. Pop. 1,186.—Also a town of Norway, in the prov. of N Berghen, on the N bank of the estuary of the Sogne-fiord, 50 m. N of Bergen. -Also a town of Norway, in the amt of Bradsberg, on the r. bank of the Flodals, 20 m. WNW of Skien.
BOEGE, a town of Savoy, in the prov. of Faucigny, 9 m. N of Bonneville, and 12 m. E of Geneva. It has three annual fairs, the trade of which consists chiefly in cattle and mules. Pop. 1,100.

BOEHMERWALD, or Bohemian Forest, [Bo- HEMIAN, Szumava, a chain of mountains in Germany, which, from the Fichtelberg, its point of junction with the Erzegebirge, extends, in a direction generally from NW to SE, from the sources of the Eger, in 50° 2' N lat., and 11° 55' E long., to the rise of the Moravian mountains, in 49° N lat., and 13° 15' E long., at the knot whence the different sources of the Taya and of the Luschnitz flow. This chain separates the basin of the Elbe from that of the Danube; and makes part of that series of high lands which establishes in Europe the division of the waters flowing, on one side, towards the Baltic, the North sea, and the Atlantic; and on the other towards the Mediterranean, the Black sea, and the Caspian. Its ridge marks on many points the limit of Bohemia with Bavaria to the SW, and with the archduchy of Austria to the S. From the Fichtelberg, this ridge directs itself first to the E, between the Eger and the Nab, as far as 12° 20' E long.; then to the SE, forming some small windings; then to the E, to the source of the Kamp; and finally to the NE, to its junction with the Moravian mountains. Its extent is 206 geog. m.; its breadth towards the NW extremity is 12½ geog. m.; at its centre 20 geog. m.; and at the S end upwards of 30 geog. m. This ridge at its first outset diminishes in height to the point where it turns to the SE; then it rises as far as the passage of the road from Ratisbon to Pilsen, by Waldmunchen. It attains its greatest height in 13° 20' E long., between that passage and the source of the Muldau. It diminishes in height greatly after leaving this point; and where it joins the Moravian ridge, it is nothing more than the back of an undulating region. The highest summits are the Untersberg, 859 Vienna toises or 5,897 ft.; the Arberg, in 49° 6' N lat., at the sources of the Regen, 4,437 ft.; the Rachel, between those of the Great Regen and the Ils, 4,048 ft.; the Dreyssel, near those of the Moldau, 2,975 ft.; the Copper-chaper or Kuffer-capelle, 2,883 ft.; the Bierhugel, 2,865 ft.; and Einseidel, in the circle of Pilsen, 2,757 ft. On the side of Bohemia, the slopes of this chain are gentlest; but towards Bavaria and Austria the declivity is very steep. In general the Boehmerwald, whose base is granite covered with clayey schist, presents steep summits, needles, profound gulfs, and numerous marshes. The dense forests which cover it serve as retreats for bears and lynxes. "The high valleys only that run up amidst the spurs of the mountains, scantily tilled, support a few robust and simple men, half-foresters, half-peasants, little changed in speech or habits from the rudeness of ancient times, and still living with undisturbed faith in the customs and traditions of the past. They seldom wander beyond the limits of this secluded region, and know little of strangers from the busier world beyond it. Here, then," says a recent and evidently well-informed writer, "may still be found, in undisturbed power, the awfulness, as well as the beauty, of forest and mountain scenery; the deep impressiveness of which is to the German what the ocean influences are to the English poet. It is a region of exceeding loveliness, encircled with the mystery of solitude; where the solemn grandeur of nature is spread out in vast but not ungracious outlines. Green valleys nestle amidst the tufted precipices; the upland glades often spread their bosoms to form the basin of lakes dark with mountain and forest shadows; the woods, too, are musical with the falls of trickling waters, bright as the dew; and the trees that cover the ruggedness of primeval rocks grow up stately and umbrageous, in a climate that is mild and delicious all through the summer months, and in winter shelter from the mountain air the clearings and valleys, which they embrace with a perennial vegetation. It is one of the few districts now remaining in the heart of modern Europe where nature may still be found slumbering in majestic proportions, unchanged by the quick industry of man; where a sensitive, dreamy lover of her looks and voices may still busy himself, and for a time forget the restless work of human energies and human wants, that has nearly effaced her original features from the Europe of our day." The Eger, which proceeds from the point of junction of the Erzegebirge and the Boehmerwald, is, with the Moldau, the only affluent which this chain furnishes directly to the Elbe. The other rivers worthy of notice, such as the Beraun, the Woltawa, and the Luschnitz, descend also from the same side; but they are mere tributaries of the Moldau. Amongst the number of branches which the B. detaches into Bohemia, may be cited the principal branch, which runs E and W, and divides the affluents on the r. bank of the Eger from the numerous tributaries of the Moldau; the Braywald, which runs NE from the left of the Woltawa to the S of the Beraun; the Lissiwald, which runs SE from the source and from the left of the Moldau, as far as BOG towards Krumau, and the branches of which diminish to the N, on the Woltawa. The Nab, the Regen, the Ilz, the Kamp, the Taya, and some other less considerable streams, furrow the SW and SE sides, and fall into the Danube, with the exception, however, of the Taya, which does not fall into that river but by the intermediation of the Morawa. These streams are separated by branches projected from the main range, of which the most remarkable are the Baierwald, which is detached from the Rachel, and which bifurcates, one branch going to the SW, near Ratisbon, and the other S, as far as Passau. The Greinerwald follows the r. bank of the Kamp, and terminates with it. Between the Taya and the Kamp, and particularly to the left of the latter stream, with which it describes an arch, the Wildgebirge is seen to extend.
BOEKEL, a town of Holland, in the prov. of N Brabant, 17 m. ESE of Bois-le-Duc.

BOEN, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Loire, arrond. of Montbrison.-The cant. comprises 22 com., and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 12,585.—The town is on the l. bank of the Lignon, 9 m. N of Montbrison. It contains some paper-mills, and has a considerable commerce in wine, the produce of the locality, corn, and timber. Pop. 1,438.
BOENZA. See SAINT SALVADOR.
BOEO (CAPE), a headland of Sicily, in the dist. of Trepani, 11 m. W of Marsala, forming the most westerly angle of the island. It is the celebrated Lilybæum Promontorium of the Romans.
BOERSCH, or Bersch, a commune and town of France, in the prov. of the Lower Rhine, cant. of Rosheim, 18 m. N of Schelestat. Pop. 2,345.
BOESCHEN. See BISCHHEIM.
BOESCHEPE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Steenvoorde. Pop. 1,938.
BOESDORF, a town of Oldenburg, in a detached portion of the duchy within the prov. of Holstein, 23 m. NNW of Lubeck.

BOESINGHE, a canal of Belgium, in the prov. of W Flanders, extending from Ypres to the fort of Knocke, where, after a course of 12 m., it unites with the Yperlee by means of a sluice, remarkable for the ingenuity of its construction.-Also a commune <math>3\frac{1}{2}</math> m. N of Ypres. Pop. 1,789.
BOETIS. See GUADALQUIVER.

BOEUFF, a town of Franklin co., U.S., in the state of Missouri. Pop. 1,662.—Also a river which, after a course of about 180 m. through the states of Arkansas and Louisiana, unites with the Washita in N lat. 31° 47′, 10 m. N of Harrisburg. BOEUFF, BAYOU, or CREEK OF LOUISIANA, an inosculation of the Red river and the Atchafalaya, in the state of Louisiana, U.S. It separates from the former at Alexandria, in Rapide par., is joined by the Bayou de Crocodile in St. Landre par., and thence taking the name of Courta-bleau, enters the Atchafalaya 18 m. ESE of Opelousas. To the E of Cheneyville it sends off a branch which reaches the Atchafalaya, after a circuitous course through Avoyelles par. The banks of the B. are fertile.
BOEVO, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 50 m. NE of Mohilev, on an affluent of the Dnieper, 25 m. E of Orcha.
BOFFIN, a lake in co. Galway, in the district of Cunnemara, 4½ m. W of Oughterard.
BOFFRES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ardeche, cant. of Vernoux. Pop. 1,618.

BOG, a river of Russia, a tributary of the Dnieper, which rises 6 m. SSW of Staro-Constantinov in Volhynia; receives the Ingul, Balta, Tshertal, and Salonika in Podolia; flows SE to Nikolaieffsk, watering Bratzla, Bohopol, Olviopol, and Vosnesensk; descends near Sekolnie into the low ground extending along the r. bank of the Dnieper, where it spreads out into a liman of nearly 50 m. in length; and joins the Dnieper to the E of Oczakof, after a course of 450 m. It receives the Seninka or Siniuka at Olviopol. Its current, except when swollen by rains, is slow, particularly in the lower part of its course.
BOG. See Bug.

BOGAN, a river of Australia, rising in Hervey's range, and the elevated country between the Lachlan and the Macquarie, and flowing W with a steady current of 4 m. per hour [in Sept.], to its junction with the Darling, after a course of 250 m.
BOGARIAH, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bengal, and dist. and 25 m. SSW of Boglipoor, on the l. bank of the Goga.
BOGARRAH, or BIGERRA, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. of La Mancha, near the r. bank of the Alamedas, 27 m. ESE of Alcaraz.
BOGASEN, a town of Prussia, in the duchy and 24 m. N of Posen, on the l. bank of the Welna, 27 m. NW of Gnesen.
BOGASHUA CAPE, a promontory on the S coast of Arabia, in the prov. of Hadramaut in N lat. 14° 6′, E long. 50°. The contiguous land is high.
BOGASIE, a river of S Africa, in the Kaffir territory, which enters the Indian ocean to the S of the bay of Lorenzo Marquez.
BOGATOI, or BOGATYI, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 50 m. S of Kursk, in the dist. and 20 m. SSW of Oboyane, on the Pena. Pop. in 1833, 182.
BOGDAN. See MOLDAVIA.
BOGDANOVKA, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of the Taurida, on the r. bank of the Molotchnonia, 47 m. SSE of Alexandrovsk. Pop. 1,150. It is situated in a sterile district.
BOGDANY, a town of Hungary, in the Saboltscher comitat, 36 m. NNE of Debreczin.

BOGDA GORA, or Bogdo Oola, a hill of rocksalt in Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Astrakan, 40 m. E of Tchernoi-Jar. It is an isolated peak, in the midst of an immense steppe; and from the supposed resemblance of its outline to a lion couchant, is sometimes called Arslan-Nla, or Lion's Mount.
BOGDA. See BOKDA.

BOGDINSKOI, or Baskoutchatskoe, a lake of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Astrakan, and dist. of Tchernoi-Jar, at the foot of the Bogda-Gora. It is 30 m. in circuit, and affords large quantities of salt of the finest description.
BOGEN, a town of Bavaria, in the presidial and 4 m. S of Mitterfels, at the junction of a river of the same name with the Danube. Pop. 1,143. It contains extensive breweries.

BOGENBERG, a village of Bavaria, in the presidial of Mitterfels, on a mountain of the same name, near the Bogen. It contains a church, one of the most resorted to as a place of pilgrimage in the kingdom, on account of an image of the Virgin of great celebrity. Pop. 218.
BOGENDORF, a village of Silesia, in the regency of Leignitz, in the circle and 18 m. WSW of Sagan. Pop. 1,300. The environs contain mines of gold, silver, copper, and lead.

BOGENHAUSEN, a village of Bavaria, 2 m. NE of Munich, on the Isar, in N lat. 48° 8' 45", and E long. 11° 36′ 42″, or 46 min. 26.5 sec. E in time of Greenwich. The royal observatory of Munich, one of the finest in Europe, was established here in 1817. It contains also a royal castle and baths. Pop. 196.

BOGENSEE, or Bovensee, a town of Denmark, in the island and diocese of Tyen, 15 m. NW of BOG 800. It possesses distilleries of brandy, and has a considerable commerce in grain. Fairs are held twice a-year. Cummin is extensively cultivated in the environs.
BOGESUND. See ULRICEHAMN.

BOGGAH, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bahar, on the l. bank of the Gunduk, 120 m. NNW of Patna. To the N of this town are extensive forests which afford excellent timber, and of which large quantities are conveyed to the dockyards of Calcutta.
BOGGAUR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bahar, 17 m. ESE of Goruckpoor.
BOGGS, a township of Clearfield co., U. S., in the state of Pennsylvania, on the main ridge of the Alleghany mountains. The soil consists of slate and gravelly limestone. Pop. in 1840, 295.

BOGHELA, or BAGHELCUND, a district of Hindostan, in the prov. of Gundwana, watered by the Sone and Mahana. It produces wheat, barley, &c., but is very inadequately cultivated, the rearing of sheep and cattle forming the chief occupation of the inhabitants. This dist. is under the protection of, and to some extent tributary to, the British government. Its principal towns are Bandugurh and Muckondabad.
BOGKOWITZ, a town of Moravia, in the circle and 15 m. ESE of Hradisch. Pop. 1,800.

BOGLIACCO, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, at the foot of M. Castello, on the E side of Lake Garda, 16 m. NE of Salo. It consists of two parts, the most southerly of which is named Santa Pietro di Bogliacco. It is generally well built, and contains a fine villa belonging to the counts of Petrini, with orange and citron gardens of great extent.
BOGLIO, or Beuil, a town of the prov. and 32 m. NW of Nice, on an affluent of the Var. Pop. 1,100.
BOGLIPUR. See BHAUGULPORE.
BOGLIUNO, a town of Illyria, in the prov. and 30 m. SSE of Trieste. It is situated in an elevated and extremely fertile dist.
